title: SSL VPN Certificate Compliance
spl:
index=vpn (sourcetype="cisco:asa" OR sourcetype="pan:system") earliest=-30d
| search "certificate" AND ("expired" OR "not trusted" OR "invalid")
| stats count by host, cert_cn, message
| sort -count
description: Tracks server certificate expiry and chain errors on SSL VPN / GlobalProtect portals from TLS handshake logs.
value: PKI and network teams monitor SSL/TLS certificate health on VPN gateways, catching expired or untrusted certificates that cause user connection failures and security warnings.
implementation: Prefer proactive cert inventory from PKI; this search catches client-reported errors. Alert on any `expired` match on production gateways.
visualization: Table (cert errors), Single value (error count), Timeline.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.user, All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Add-on for Cisco ASA and/or Palo Alto Networks Add-on. Data in `index=vpn`.
• Key fields: certificate-related syslog messages. Cisco ASA message IDs: 725002 (certificate validation), 725003 (certificate accepted), 725006 (certificate rejected).
• SSL VPN (WebVPN/AnyConnect SSL) uses server certificates for the VPN gateway and optionally client certificates for user authentication.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify certificate events:
```spl
index=vpn (sourcetype="cisco:asa" OR sourcetype="pan:system") earliest=-30d
| search "certificate" AND ("expired" OR "not trusted" OR "invalid" OR "rejected")
| stats count by host
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Certificate compliance issues:
```spl
index=vpn (sourcetype="cisco:asa" OR sourcetype="pan:system") earliest=-30d
| search "certificate" AND ("expired" OR "not trusted" OR "invalid" OR "rejected" OR "revoked")
| rex field=_raw "certificate.*?(?:subject|cn)=(?<cert_subject>[^,;]+)"
| rex field=_raw "(?:reason|error)[:=]\s*(?<cert_error>[^,;]+)"
| stats count as events dc(src) as affected_clients values(cert_error) as errors by cert_subject, host
| sort - events
```

Understanding this SPL: Identifies certificate issues from VPN gateway logs. 'Expired' certificates need immediate renewal. 'Not trusted' means the CA chain is incomplete on the client or the server cert is self-signed. 'Revoked' certificates are actively blacklisted. High `affected_clients` for a server certificate issue means all users see the warning.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Check the VPN gateway certificate: `openssl s_client -connect <vpn_host>:443 | openssl x509 -noout -dates -subject`.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — Certificate issues count. Row 2 — Table: subject, errors, affected clients.
Alert: Immediate for expired server certificates. Weekly for client cert issues.

Runbook:
1. **Server cert expired** — Emergency renewal. Until renewed, users see certificate warnings and may not be able to connect.
2. **Client cert not trusted** — Deploy the CA certificate to the client trust store via GPO/MDM.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No certificate events** — Certificate validation logging may not be at the right level. On ASA: ensure `logging trap informational`.
• **False positives from self-signed certs in lab** — Filter out known lab hostnames.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the digital certificates on our VPN servers to make sure they are valid and trusted, because expired certificates make it impossible for people to connect securely.
